01a_quotes.title: Large Language Model in Creative Work: The Role of Collaboration Modality and User Expertise

study_design: To address these gaps in our understanding, we designed a randomized experiment wherein participants were tasked with crafting advertisement copies for a common consumer product, under one of three conditions: a ghostwriter modality group, a sounding board modality group, and a control group.
Our study features a 3 (two human-LLM collaboration modalities and control group)× 2 (expert and non-expert) between-subject design.
Power analysis showed that our sample size allows a small to medium effect size of Cohen's d = 0.25 to be detected.
task: In the experiment, participants were asked to complete an ad copywriting task. Specifically, they were tasked with creating advertisements for an iPhone protective case.

To create the ad, participants are provided with detailed product descriptions and images (Figure A1a in appendix), along with a sample image of how advertisements will look on Facebook feeds (Figure A1b in appendix).

Participants were randomly assigned to three conditions: (1) utilizing AI as a ghostwriter for writing the advertisement text; (2) utilizing AI as a sounding board to solicit AI-generated feedback; and (3) no AI technology, which serves as a control group.
population: We conducted an experiment using 355 participants recruited from Prolific, a widely accepted participant recruitment platform (Palan and Schitter 2018).
To allow for greater result generalizability, participants from the United States and the United Kingdom were solicited.

Standard eligibility criteria were used to pre-screen participants, which include maintaining a minimum task acceptance rate of 95% and possessing at least a college degree (Palan and Schitter 2018, Noy and Zhang 2023).

To obtain a balanced sample of experts and non-experts, we first utilize Prolific's screening tool to identify potential experts based on the industry they have worked. Since participants from marketing & sales employment sectors are more likely to be a marketing-related knowledge, roles, and responsibilities, we recruited half of our participants from marketing & sales employment sectors, and the remaining participants were recruited from other employment sectors.
arms: Participants were randomly assigned to three conditions: (1) utilizing AI as a ghostwriter for writing the advertisement text; (2) utilizing AI as a sounding board to solicit AI-generated feedback; and (3) no AI technology, which serves as a control group.

In the sounding board condition, the LLM is prompted to solicit ad copy drafts from participants and would subsequently offer feedback for their ad copies. We designed this LLM to deny any user requests that directly ask it to produce ad copies.
In the ghostwriter condition, the LLM is prompted to compose ad copies in response to user instructions.
In the No-AI control condition, participants do not have access to the LLM interface entirely.
outcome_definitions: We adopt a commonly used definition, ad effectiveness, as measured through ad clicks, to define the quality of an ad (Naik et al. 1998, Agarwal and Mukhopadhyay 2016, Kim et al. 2019).

To obtain an objective real-world assessment of the effectiveness of the created ads, these ads were deployed on actual social media ad campaigns, to which the resultant number of clicks generated by each ad is captured and used as a dependent variable.

In addition, we have collected human-rated perceived quality of their advertisement copies. Specifically, we measured an array of items that are shown to be important factors of ad effectiveness, namely perceived informativeness (Lee et al. 2018), perceived positive affect (product interests) (Watson et al. 1988), and purchase intention (Teixeira et al. 2014).

Following each task, we asked participants to estimate the percentile rank of their advertisement among all the participant-created advertisements, using a slider ranging from 0% (worst) to 100% (best).
statistical_results: We found that experts in the control group produce ads with higher perceived quality than non-experts in the control group, wherein the average item ratings of expert-written ads (M=6.084, SD=2.027) are about 25% higher than non-experts-written ads (M=4.854, SD=2.027), p < 0.01.

One-way ANOVA tests for the age, gender and education levels revealed no significant variations (p > 0.1) across the treatment groups for these demographic features, indicating that randomization is successful.

A two-way interaction effect was observed, in which (1) non-experts benefit from AI utilization but only in the sounding board condition, while (2) experts do not benefit from AI use, regardless of the modality, with a decrease in performance observed in the ghostwriter modality.

The results show that users in both modalities significantly deemed their advertisement performance to be higher than the control group (10.6% for the sounding board group and 9.3% for the ghostwriter group), suggesting that the users of LLM have more optimistic self-evaluations.

Here, the control group has the largest semantic divergence for its ads (M=0.057, SD=0.024), indicating that users without any LLM inputs tend to produce ad copies that differ from one another within the group the most.

In contrast, the ghostwriter group has the lowest semantic divergence (M=0.031, SD=0.020) across all three groups, suggesting that anchoring effects are highly salient among participants in the ghostwriter group.
We note that the sounding board group has a lower semantic divergence (M=0.043, SD=0.026) than the control group, but significantly higher than the ghostwriter group (Figure 2).
Figure 3 shows that users in the sounding board modality revised their copies to a greater extent (M=0.040, SD=0.031) relative to users in the ghostwriter modality (M=0.029, SD=0.026), p < 0.01.
In model 2, as expected, when the first draft has a higher initial perceived quality, the participants generally make fewer revisions (β = −0.006, p < 0.01).

Even after controlling for the perceived quality of the initial draft, the collaboration modality remains a significant factor, wherein the participants in the ghostwriter group still revised less than the participants in the sounding board group (β = −0.009, p < 0.05).

Comparisons of the cosine distances (Figure 4) reveal that non-experts in the sounding board group (M=0.049, SD=0.022) produced advertisement copies that are semantically more akin (p < 0.01) to those written by reference experts, compared to the outputs generated by non-experts in the control group (M=0.055, SD=0.022) and ghostwriter group (M=0.050, SD=0.016).

In Table 4, we see that ads with more subjective sentiments—those expressing more personal opinions, emotions or judgments—tended to receive fewer clicks compared to their objective counterparts which presented facts or neutral information.
The coefficient for log(token) suggests that longer ads tend to be more effective (p < 0.001).
Additionally, Table 4 suggests that less readable ads (i.e., higher Gunning Fog Index) and ads that contain more emojis tend to perform worse.

From the summary statistics table (Table 6), we observe that users in the ghostwriter group used emojis and hashtags more frequently (p < 0.05, Table A10) compared to users from the other study groups.

We found that, in the initial draft, there were significant differences across the readability (gunning fog index), emoji usage, ad length, and hashtag usage across the two LLM treatment groups. Specifically, first drafts generated by the LLM in ghostwriter modality are less readable (higher gunning fog index, p < 0.01), include more emojis (p < 0.001), are longer in length (p < 0.001), and use more hashtags (p < 0.001), compared to the first drafts written by users in sounding board modality.

Table A6 shows that relative to users in the ghostwriter group, users in the sounding board group lengthened their ad copy considerably by 26.2 words on average (p < 0.001), as they moved from the initial draft to the last draft.

In contrast, users in the ghostwriter modality substantially reduced the length of their ads by 30.27 words (p < 0.001) in their last draft, suggesting that the initial ad produced by the LLM in the ghostwriter condition might be somewhat verbose.
Also, we see that users in the ghostwriter condition reduced the number of emojis by 2.46 (p < 0.001) on average.

Finally, Table A7 shows that these ad characteristics remain consistent across the last ad draft and the final copy in the sounding board group. However, users in the ghostwriter group exhibit some interesting patterns. On average, users in the ghostwriter group had manually reduced ad length by 5.18 words (p < 0.01), the number of emoji by 0.85 (p < 0.001), and hashtags by 0.16 (p < 0.05).
We found that experts in the ghostwriter group shortened the ad copy more than the non-experts (p < 0.05).
Experts also tend to improve the readability (or decrease the usage of more complex language) more than the non-experts (p < 0.05).
Our results (Figure 5) revealed that using LLMs as a sounding board does not improve the perceived creativity of the ads (M=4.369, SD=1.863) relative to that of the control group (M=4.521, SD=1.823).
In contrast, we learned that ad copies in the ghostwriter modality exhibit the lowest perceived creativity (M=3.974, SD=1.913) and were in fact lower than ad copies created in the control group.
We performed a subsequent analysis and found that the perceived creativity of the ads is highly correlated with the perceived quality, r(352) = 0.670, p < .001.

Model 1 in Table A2 shows that ads written by experts attract significantly more clicks than non-experts in the control group (β = 4.14, p < 0.01), indicating that our manipulation of expertise through the selection of participants using domain expertise is appropriate.
Interestingly, as shown in Model 2, the performance gap between experts and non-experts becomes non-significant when LLM is involved, suggesting that LLM helps close the performance gap.
As per Model 3, employing AI results in a marginally significant decrease in clicks (β = −1.40, p = 0.057).
Consistent with the model-free evidence, the ghostwriter condition produces a significant negative effect, wherein its usage produces three fewer ad clicks compared to the control condition.
Estimates in Model 5 reveal that the ghostwriter condition produces a significant decrease in clicks for experts compared to the control group (β = −5.07, p < 0.001).
In contrast, Model 6 demonstrates a significant increase of 2.4 advertisement clicks for non-experts employing LLM as a sounding board compared to the control group (p < 0.05).
One-way ANOVA analysis also shows that the impressions are not statistically significantly different across treatment conditions (p > 0.1).
ambiguity_or_caveats: While the versatility of LLM has unlocked new ways of human-AI collaboration, it remains uncertain how LLMs should be used to enhance business outcomes.
Although insightful, the codifiable tasks studied previously were quite different from the tasks that LLMs are capable of assisting with.
Answers to the impact of human-LLM collaboration on work performance are shrouded in uncertainty due to the presence of competing theoretical perspectives.
However, due to their autoregressive nature, LLMs may be limited to generating expected outputs as they are trained to adhere to existing data distributions (Bunescu and Uduehi 2019).
Consequently, having LLMs take the lead in the creative process may not be optimal.
Nonetheless, the quality of the LLM-generated feedback and the extent to which users can effectively integrate such feedback into their work remain uncertain.
In particular, users may not fully exploit the benefits of AI because of difficulties in evaluating the correctness of AI advice (Jussupow et al. 2021).

The rationale behind the choice of this product is that it is a common consumer product that does not require industry-specific knowledge to perform the task, making most research participants solicited online adequate candidates.
It is plausible that the same viewer may see multiple ads crafted by different experiment participants because of the retargeting algorithm, thereby obfuscating the contribution of each ad.
For instance, some participants did not ask the LLM to provide further advice (sounding board group) or did not use LLM (ghostwriter group).
It is possible that using LLM as a ghostwriter may produce a more complete and high-quality first draft, reducing the need for further revisions.

A deeper examination of the existing work on the effectiveness of emojis (e.g., Das et al. 2019) revealed that the study was conducted at least six years prior to our study. However, a newer study published in 2023 found that usage of emojis in messages decreases message credibility and source trustworthiness (Koch et al. 2023) which can reduce the willingness to click on such ads.
Given the LLM is trained on past data and is not optimized with newer data, it fails to recognize that the use of emojis may not resonate well with consumers more recently.

The lack of performance improvement among experts when using LLMs as a sounding board could be due to a "ceiling effect," wherein the performance of general-purpose LLMs is already near the upper limit of ad performance in our experiment.

Moreover, general-purpose LLMs are trained using a variety of web content (OpenAI 2024) which include advertising content and non-marketing content. That is, they are not trained exclusively with ad content of excellent quality.

Thus, while non-experts are able to improve their ad-writing skills from the feedback offered by the LLM tool, experts are unlikely to surpass the global level of ad-writing skills gleaned from the LLM, as they already possess these marketing knowledge and skill sets.

01a_quotes.notes: This is an empirical randomized controlled experiment with 355 participants assigned to three conditions (ghostwriter, sounding board, control) crossed with expertise level (expert vs. non-expert). The primary outcome is ad clicks from real social media campaigns. Secondary outcomes include semantic divergence analysis, textual characteristics, and perceived creativity ratings. The paper includes extensive mechanism analyses using text embeddings and qualitative feedback.

01_extraction.title: Large Language Model in Creative Work: The Role of Collaboration Modality and User Expertise
01_extraction.study_design_description: A randomized experiment with a 3 (two human-LLM collaboration modalities and control group) × 2 (expert and non-expert) between-subject design. Participants were tasked with crafting advertisement copies for an iPhone protective case under one of three conditions: a ghostwriter modality group, a sounding board modality group, and a control group. Power analysis showed that the sample size allows a small to medium effect size of Cohen's d = 0.25 to be detected.
01_extraction.primary_task_description: Participants were asked to complete an ad copywriting task, specifically creating advertisements for an iPhone protective case. Participants were provided with detailed product descriptions and images, along with a sample image of how advertisements would look on Facebook feeds. Participants were randomly assigned to three conditions: (1) utilizing AI as a ghostwriter for writing the advertisement text; (2) utilizing AI as a sounding board to solicit AI-generated feedback; and (3) no AI technology, which serves as a control group.
01_extraction.population_description: 355 participants recruited from Prolific, a participant recruitment platform. Participants were from the United States and the United Kingdom. Standard eligibility criteria included maintaining a minimum task acceptance rate of 95% and possessing at least a college degree. To obtain a balanced sample of experts and non-experts, half of the participants were recruited from marketing & sales employment sectors, and the remaining participants were recruited from other employment sectors.

arms.description: No AI technology; participants do not have access to the LLM interface entirely.

The LLM is prompted to solicit ad copy drafts from participants and would subsequently offer feedback for their ad copies. The LLM is designed to deny any user requests that directly ask it to produce ad copies.
The LLM is prompted to compose ad copies in response to user instructions.

outcomes.outcome_description: Ad effectiveness as measured through ad clicks generated by each ad deployed on actual social media ad campaigns.
Human-rated perceived quality of advertisement copies, measured through an array of items including perceived informativeness, perceived positive affect (product interests), and purchase intention.
Perceived creativity of the ads as rated by participants.
Measure of how much ad copies differ from one another within a group, calculated using semantic similarity metrics.
The extent to which users revised their ad copies from initial draft to final version.
Cosine distance measuring how semantically similar non-expert-written ads are to those written by reference experts.
Change in the number of words in ad copies from initial draft to final version.
Change in the number of emojis used in ad copies from initial draft to final version.
Change in the number of hashtags used in ad copies from initial draft to final version.
Readability of ad copies as measured by Gunning Fog Index.
Participants' estimated percentile rank of their advertisement among all participant-created advertisements.
Number of impressions generated by ads on social media campaigns.

01_extraction.notes: This is a 3×2 factorial randomized controlled experiment with 355 participants (N=117 control, N=122 sounding board, N=116 ghostwriter) crossed with expertise level (expert vs. non-expert). The primary outcome is ad clicks from real social media campaigns. Secondary outcomes include perceived quality, perceived creativity, semantic divergence, revision extent, semantic alignment to experts, and various textual characteristics (length, emoji usage, hashtag usage, readability). The paper reports extensive mechanism analyses using text embeddings and qualitative feedback. Key finding: non-experts benefit from AI as a sounding board (2.4 more clicks, p < 0.05), while experts show no benefit and actually perform worse with ghostwriter modality (5.07 fewer clicks, p < 0.001). The paper notes several caveats including potential retargeting algorithm effects on ad attribution, non-compliance in some conditions, and the possibility of ceiling effects for expert performance.

02_method.rationale: The study uses a randomized between-subject design with participants recruited from Prolific completing an online task (crafting advertisement copies). This is randomized but neither a controlled lab setting nor a real work setting, fitting the 'rct' category for online randomized experiments.

per_outcome.operationalization: number of clicks generated by each ad when deployed in actual Facebook ad campaigns
human ratings on informativeness, positive affect, and purchase intention, aggregated into quality score
participant ratings of ad creativity on rubric scale
cosine distance measuring semantic similarity among ads within a treatment group; diversity metric, not speed or quality of individual outputs
magnitude of revisions from initial draft to final version; process measure, not output speed or quality
cosine distance between non-expert ads and reference expert-written ads
change in word count from initial to final draft; process measure, not output quality or speed
change in emoji count from initial to final draft; process measure, not output quality or speed
change in hashtag count from initial to final draft; process measure, not output quality or speed
Gunning Fog Index score computed from final ad text
participant self-reported estimated percentile rank of their ad among all participant ads; subjective confidence, not objective quality
count of impressions generated in social media campaigns; reach metric, not quality of ad content

04_onet.rationale: The participants' substantive task is 'creating advertisements for an iPhone protective case' — a creative content generation activity. While the study manipulates AI assistance (ghostwriter, feedback, or none), the core work being performed by participants is creative ad copywriting. This maps most directly to 'Thinking Creatively' (WA-13), which encompasses developing original ideas and creative content. The task is narrowly defined (writing ad copy) rather than representing the full scope of a marketing occupation, making a work activity mapping more appropriate than an occupational mapping.

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: The study has two AI arms: 'Sounding board' and 'Ghostwriter'. The sounding board modality shows positive effects for non-experts (2.296 more clicks, p<0.05) while ghostwriter shows negative effects overall (-3.014 clicks, p<0.001). Since the research question focuses on beneficial AI collaboration, and sounding board represents a more successful modality, I select it as the best AI arm. Table 1 provides summary statistics: No-AI has M=16.2, SD=6.6, N=117; Sounding board has M=16.4, SD=6.2, N=122.
llm_extracted.notes: Summary statistics extracted from Table 1 on page 13. The table provides mean ad clicks and standard deviations for each condition. No-AI (Control): Mean=16.2, SD=6.6, N=117. Sounding board: Mean=16.4, SD=6.2, N=122. Ghostwriter: Mean=13.0, SD=5.0, N=116. The prior extraction missed these descriptive statistics which are clearly presented in the summary table. The regression models in Table A2 show the sounding board effect is not significant overall (β=0.140, p>0.1 in Model 4) but is significant for non-experts specifically (β=2.296, p<0.05 in Model 5). The means and SDs allow computation of Hedges' g using Level A data.
source_quotes: Table 1: Summary Statistics - No-AI (N=117) Mean Std. Dev. ... Ad Clicks 16.2 6.6
Table 1: Summary Statistics - Sounding board (N=122) Mean Std. Dev. ... Ad Clicks 16.4 6.2
Model 6 demonstrates a significant increase of 2.4 advertisement clicks for non-experts employing LLM as a sounding board compared to the control group (p < 0.05).

computed.notes: Summary statistics extracted from Table 1 on page 13. The table provides mean ad clicks and standard deviations for each condition. No-AI (Control): Mean=16.2, SD=6.6, N=117. Sounding board: Mean=16.4, SD=6.2, N=122. Ghostwriter: Mean=13.0, SD=5.0, N=116. The prior extraction missed these descriptive statistics which are clearly presented in the summary table. The regression models in Table A2 show the sounding board effect is not significant overall (β=0.140, p>0.1 in Model 4) but is significant for non-experts specifically (β=2.296, p<0.05 in Model 5). The means and SDs allow computation of Hedges' g using Level A data.
